title: International tourist arrivals in three Southeast Asian countries, 2012-2022
question_text: The line chart below shows the number of international tourist arrivals (in millions) in Vietnam, Thailand and Malaysia from 2012 to 2022. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.
sample_band_7_answer:
The line chart illustrates the number of international tourists, measured in millions, visiting Vietnam, Thailand and Malaysia between 2012 and 2022.

Overall, all three countries experienced a steady increase in tourist numbers until 2019, followed by a sharp decline in 2020. Thailand consistently attracted the largest number of visitors during the pre-pandemic period, while Vietnam saw the fastest relative growth.

In 2012, Malaysia received approximately 25 million tourists, slightly more than Thailand at 22.4 million, whereas Vietnam lagged far behind at only 6.8 million. Over the next seven years, Thailand's figures surged to nearly 40 million, while Vietnam more than doubled to 18 million by 2019. Malaysia, by contrast, remained relatively stable at around 26 million.

The year 2020 marked a dramatic turning point, with all three countries witnessing a collapse in tourist numbers due to the pandemic. Figures plummeted to 6.7 million in Thailand, 4.3 million in Malaysia and 3.7 million in Vietnam. By 2022, Malaysia and Thailand had begun to recover, reaching 10.1 and 11.2 million respectively, whereas Vietnam's tourism remained stagnant at 3.6 million.
feature_hints: Overall upward trend until 2019 across all three countries
Sharp collapse in 2020 affecting all three equally
Thailand consistently highest; Vietnam consistently lowest until pandemic
Vietnam showed fastest relative growth (nearly tripled 2012-2019)
Malaysia remained most stable before pandemic

title: Mobile phone subscriptions per 100 people in four countries, 2005-2020
question_text: The line chart below shows the number of mobile phone subscriptions per 100 people in the United States, Japan, Brazil and India from 2005 to 2020. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.
sample_band_7_answer:
The line chart compares mobile phone subscription rates per 100 inhabitants in the United States, Japan, Brazil and India over a fifteen-year period from 2005 to 2020.

Overall, subscription rates rose significantly in all four countries, with India experiencing the most dramatic growth. Japan emerged as the country with the highest penetration by 2020, while the United States and Brazil saw modest declines in the final period.

In 2005, Japan led with 76 subscriptions per 100 people, closely followed by the United States at 69, while Brazil stood at 46 and India lagged far behind at only 8. Between 2005 and 2015, all four nations experienced rapid expansion. Brazil surged from 46 to 126, overtaking the United States at 118, while Japan reached 127. India, despite remaining the lowest, climbed nearly tenfold to 78.

From 2015 to 2020, trends diverged. Japan continued to rise sharply to 146, whereas the United States and Brazil declined to 107 and 99 respectively. India, meanwhile, registered a modest increase to 85 by the end of the period.
feature_hints: Japan highest by 2020 at 146 per 100 people
India showed most dramatic growth (8 to 85)
US and Brazil declined after 2015 peak
All countries started at very different baselines
Gap between developed and developing narrowed significantly

title: CO2 emissions per capita in three countries, 1990-2020
question_text: The line chart below shows carbon dioxide emissions per capita (in metric tonnes) in China, the United Kingdom and the United States between 1990 and 2020. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.
sample_band_7_answer:
The line graph illustrates per capita carbon dioxide emissions, measured in metric tonnes, in the United States, the United Kingdom and China across a thirty-year span from 1990 to 2020.

Overall, the two developed nations saw their emissions fall substantially over the period, whereas China's figures climbed considerably. Despite this shift, American emissions per person remained the highest throughout.

In 1990, the United States produced 19.5 tonnes per capita, almost double the UK's 10 tonnes and nearly nine times China's modest 2.2 tonnes. By 2000, US emissions had risen slightly to a peak of 20.5 tonnes, while the UK declined marginally to 9.2. China, meanwhile, experienced a small increase to 2.7 tonnes.

The following two decades revealed contrasting trajectories. American and British emissions both fell steadily, reaching 14.2 and 4.9 tonnes respectively by 2020. In stark contrast, China's per capita figure tripled between 2000 and 2010 to 6.8 tonnes and continued inching upwards to 7.4 by the end of the period, surpassing the UK.
feature_hints: US highest throughout all three decades
UK and US declined; China rose substantially
US peak in 2000 then steady decline
Gap between US and others narrowed over time

title: Birth rate and death rate in Vietnam, 1980-2020
question_text: The line chart below shows the birth rate and death rate per 1,000 people in Vietnam from 1980 to 2020. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.
sample_band_7_answer:
The line chart illustrates the birth rate and death rate per 1,000 people in Vietnam over four decades, from 1980 to 2020.

Overall, the birth rate declined dramatically during this period, while the death rate remained relatively stable after an initial decrease. The gap between the two rates narrowed significantly by 2020.

In 1980, Vietnam's birth rate stood at 35.7 per 1,000, more than four times higher than the death rate of 8 per 1,000. The birth rate then fell gradually to 30.9 by 1990, before dropping sharply to 19 per 1,000 in 2000. This rapid decline continued at a slower pace afterwards, reaching 17.2 in 2010 and settling at 16.3 by 2020.

The death rate, in contrast, showed a very different pattern. It decreased gently from 8 in 1980 to a low point of 5.8 in 2000. From that year onward, the figure edged up slightly, reaching 6.1 in 2010 and 6.4 in 2020. Despite this modest rise, the death rate in 2020 remained considerably lower than its 1980 level.
feature_hints: Gap between the two narrowed significantly
Death rate lowest in 2000 then slight rise

title: Number of international students enrolled in universities in four countries, 2010-2022
question_text: The line chart below shows the number of international students (in thousands) enrolled in universities in the United States, the United Kingdom, Australia and Canada between 2010 and 2022. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.
sample_band_7_answer:
The line chart presents the number of international university students, in thousands, hosted by four English-speaking destinations between 2010 and 2022.

Overall, all four countries witnessed growth in their international student populations, although patterns varied considerably. The United States remained the leading destination, while Canada emerged as the fastest-growing host.

The United States began the period with 690 thousand international students and climbed steadily to a peak of 1,094 thousand in 2018. This figure then fell to 914 thousand in 2020 before recovering modestly to 948 thousand by 2022. The United Kingdom followed a smoother upward path, rising gradually from 405 thousand in 2010 to 458 thousand in 2018, then surging to 680 thousand by 2022.

Canada's growth was particularly striking. Starting from the lowest base of 212 thousand, it nearly tripled to 572 thousand by 2018 and reached 807 thousand in 2022, overtaking the UK. Australia, meanwhile, followed an erratic trajectory: its figures dipped to 296 thousand in 2014, peaked at 524 thousand in 2018, and subsequently declined to 419 thousand in 2022, ending as the lowest of the four.
feature_hints: Australia showed erratic pattern with decline post-2018

title: Life expectancy at birth in three regions, 1960-2020
question_text: The line chart below shows life expectancy at birth, in years, in Sub-Saharan Africa, Latin America and East Asia from 1960 to 2020. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.
sample_band_7_answer:
The line chart depicts the change in average life expectancy at birth across three world regions — East Asia, Latin America and Sub-Saharan Africa — over a sixty-year period from 1960 to 2020.

Overall, life expectancy rose substantially in all three regions, although the pace of improvement differed. East Asia achieved the most impressive gains, overtaking Latin America to claim the highest figure by 2020.

In 1960, Latin America led with an average life expectancy of 56 years, while East Asia and Sub-Saharan Africa were roughly level at 42 and 40 years respectively. Between 1960 and 1980, East Asia advanced rapidly to match Latin America at 65 years. Sub-Saharan Africa, in contrast, made only modest progress, reaching 48 years.

In the final four decades, East Asia continued to outpace the other regions, climbing to 77 years by 2020. Latin America followed closely, increasing to 75 years, while Sub-Saharan Africa, despite lagging well behind throughout, saw its life expectancy rise from 50 to 62 years between 2000 and 2020 — the fastest gain in any single 20-year period for that region.
feature_hints: East Asia gained most overall (42 to 77 years)
East Asia overtook Latin America between 2000-2020

title: Average annual crude oil price, 2000-2022 (USD per barrel)
question_text: The line chart below shows the average annual price of crude oil (in US dollars per barrel) between 2000 and 2022. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.
sample_band_7_answer:
The line chart illustrates fluctuations in the average annual price of crude oil, measured in US dollars per barrel, across a 22-year period from 2000 to 2022.

Overall, oil prices exhibited considerable volatility, with sharp peaks in 2008, 2012 and 2022 interspersed with dramatic slumps. Despite these oscillations, the price in 2022 was almost four times higher than the starting figure.

At the turn of the millennium, crude oil was relatively cheap at 28 dollars per barrel, rising gradually to 38 dollars by 2004. Prices then soared to a striking peak of 97 dollars in 2008, only to collapse to 62 dollars the following year amid the global financial crisis.

A strong rebound pushed the price to an all-time high of 112 dollars per barrel in 2012, after which a prolonged downward trend set in. By 2016, the figure had plummeted to 43 dollars, and it remained subdued at 42 dollars in 2020 owing to pandemic-related demand shocks. The final two years saw a dramatic resurgence, with oil climbing steeply to 101 dollars by 2022 — a level not seen for a decade.
feature_hints: Overall upward trend with huge volatility

title: Average monthly household expenditure in an Asian country, 2010 vs 2020
question_text: The bar chart below shows the average monthly household expenditure (in US dollars) on five categories in an Asian country in 2010 and 2020. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.
sample_band_7_answer:
The bar chart compares the average monthly household spending on five categories — food, housing, transport, education and entertainment — in an Asian country, in 2010 and 2020.

Overall, expenditure rose across every category during the decade, with housing becoming the single largest area of spending by 2020. The most significant relative increase occurred in entertainment, while housing showed the greatest absolute rise.

In 2010, food accounted for the highest level of spending at 180 dollars per month, followed by housing at 150 dollars and transport at 80 dollars. Education and entertainment trailed behind at 60 and 40 dollars respectively.

By 2020, the picture had shifted markedly. Housing climbed to 290 dollars, overtaking food at 240 dollars to become the top spending category. Transport doubled to 160 dollars, and education rose from 60 to 140 dollars — more than twice its 2010 level. Entertainment showed the steepest proportional growth, nearly tripling from 40 to 110 dollars, although it remained the smallest category overall. These changes suggest households increasingly directed their budgets towards essential services and leisure as incomes rose.
feature_hints: Housing overtook food as largest category
Entertainment showed largest relative increase (nearly tripled)

title: Percentage of adults owning a smartphone by age group in 2015 and 2023
question_text: The bar chart below shows the percentage of adults in a European country who owned a smartphone, broken down by age group, in 2015 and 2023. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.
sample_band_7_answer:
The bar chart illustrates the percentage of adults who owned a smartphone in a European country, broken down into four age groups, in 2015 and 2023.

Overall, smartphone ownership rose across every age category over the eight-year period. While younger adults already had high ownership rates in 2015, the most dramatic gains were recorded among older age groups.

In 2015, smartphone ownership was strongly age-related. Young adults aged 18 to 29 led the way at 85 per cent, followed by the 30-to-49 group at 75 per cent. Middle-aged adults aged 50 to 64 had a more moderate ownership rate of 52 per cent, while only 18 per cent of those aged 65 and over reported owning a smartphone.

By 2023, the differences between age groups had narrowed considerably. Nearly all younger adults (98 per cent) and those aged 30 to 49 (95 per cent) owned a smartphone, while ownership among the 50-to-64 category rose sharply to 85 per cent. Most striking was the change among the oldest group, whose ownership more than tripled from 18 to 62 per cent — the largest absolute rise of any age bracket.
feature_hints: Gap between youngest and oldest narrowed

title: Value of exports in five sectors for a South Asian country, 2015 and 2022
question_text: The bar chart below shows the value of exports (in billion US dollars) from five sectors of a South Asian economy in 2015 and 2022. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.
sample_band_7_answer:
The bar chart compares the export values, in billions of US dollars, of five sectors in a South Asian economy between 2015 and 2022.

Overall, every sector registered higher export revenues over the seven-year period. Textiles remained the single largest earner, but pharmaceuticals and electronics expanded most rapidly and began to rival the traditional exports.

In 2015, textiles dominated the country's exports at 28 billion dollars, well ahead of agriculture at 18 billion and machinery at 12 billion. Pharmaceuticals and electronics contributed comparatively little, at 9 and 6 billion dollars respectively.

By 2022, the export landscape had diversified considerably. Textiles climbed to 42 billion dollars, maintaining its leading position, while agriculture grew modestly to 22 billion. The more striking changes occurred in three other sectors: machinery more than doubled to 28 billion, pharmaceuticals nearly tripled from 9 to 25 billion, and electronics expanded fourfold from 6 to 24 billion. As a result, the gap between the top and bottom sectors narrowed significantly, and the country appears to have shifted towards a more diversified export base.

title: Employment rate of recent graduates by field of study, 2022
question_text: The bar chart below shows the employment rate (percentage employed within six months of graduation) for recent graduates in five fields of study, across three countries, in 2022. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.
sample_band_7_answer:
The bar chart compares the proportion of recent graduates in five disciplines who secured employment within six months in Germany, South Korea and Mexico in 2022.

Overall, Germany reported the highest employment rates across all fields, whereas Mexico consistently ranked lowest. Computer science produced the strongest outcomes in every country, while arts graduates faced the greatest difficulty entering the workforce.

In Germany, graduates performed strongly across the board. Computer science led the way at 95 per cent, closely followed by engineering at 92 per cent and business at 88 per cent. Education reached 85 per cent, while arts stood at 70 per cent — still the highest figure for that field among the three countries.

South Korea showed a broadly similar pattern but at lower absolute levels, with computer science and engineering topping 85 per cent and arts at just 58 per cent. In Mexico, the gap between fields was the widest. Computer science performed reasonably well at 82 per cent, but business and education trailed at 65 and 68 per cent respectively. Arts graduates in Mexico faced the toughest prospects, with less than half — only 45 per cent — in employment.
feature_hints: Computer science highest in each country

title: Average daily screen time by purpose, four countries, 2023
question_text: The bar chart below shows the average daily screen time, in hours, by adults in four countries for three purposes — work, entertainment and social media — in 2023. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.
sample_band_7_answer:
The bar chart shows how many hours adults in the United States, Japan, Brazil and Nigeria spent in front of screens each day in 2023, split across three activities: work, entertainment and social media.

Overall, Japanese adults recorded the longest screen time for work, while Brazilians dedicated the most hours to both entertainment and social media. Nigerians registered the lowest total screen time among the four countries.

Workplace screen use dominated in the two higher-income countries. Japanese adults spent 6.1 hours per day in front of work-related screens, the highest figure in the chart, closely followed by Americans at 5.2 hours. By contrast, Brazilians and Nigerians used screens for work considerably less, at 3.8 and 3.2 hours respectively.

Entertainment patterns were less clear-cut. Brazilians led at 4.2 hours a day, ahead of Americans at 3.4 hours, while Japanese and Nigerians watched rather less, at 2.8 and 2.6 hours. Social media showed a striking contrast: Brazilians (3.7 hours) and Nigerians (3.5 hours) spent over twice as long as the Japanese (only 1.3 hours), with Americans falling in between at 2.5 hours.
feature_hints: Brazil highest for entertainment and social media
Social media gap huge: Brazil/Nigeria ~3x Japan
Work dominant in US and Japan

title: Per capita carbon footprint by sector in five countries, 2022
question_text: The bar chart below shows the per capita carbon footprint (in tonnes of CO2 equivalent) from four sectors — transport, housing, food and other consumption — across five countries in 2022. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.
sample_band_7_answer:
The bar chart illustrates the per capita carbon emissions, measured in tonnes of CO2 equivalent, generated by four consumption sectors in five countries during 2022.

Overall, residents of the United States were responsible for by far the highest emissions across every sector, whereas those in Kenya contributed negligible amounts. Transport and household energy emerged as the two most significant sources of emissions in wealthier nations.

Americans produced a substantial 13.5 tonnes per person when the four sectors are combined, with transport alone accounting for 4.8 tonnes — more than the entire carbon footprint of the average Indian or Kenyan citizen. Housing (3.1 tonnes) and other consumption (3.6 tonnes) also contributed heavily. Germans followed at roughly 8.2 tonnes overall, with a broadly even spread across the four categories.

China, now an industrial powerhouse, registered 6.6 tonnes per capita, dominated by housing at 2.5 tonnes, reflecting its coal-heavy electricity mix. The two lowest-emitting nations, India and Kenya, generated only 2.7 and 1.4 tonnes respectively, with food and housing representing their principal sources. Kenya's transport emissions were particularly minimal at just 0.2 tonnes per person.
feature_hints: US highest across every sector (13.5 total)
Kenya lowest across every sector (1.4 total)
Developing countries food-heavy relative to total
German per capita about 60% of US

title: Preferred mode of commuting to work in a European city, 2010 and 2023
question_text: The bar chart below shows the percentage of workers in a European city using five different modes of transport to commute to work in 2010 and 2023. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.
sample_band_7_answer:
The bar chart compares the proportion of workers in a European city who used five different modes of commuting in 2010 and 2023.

Overall, reliance on the private car fell sharply over the period, while cycling, public transport and remote working all gained ground. The commuting pattern became noticeably more diverse by 2023.

In 2010, the private car dominated commuting, with 55 per cent of workers travelling to work this way. Public transport came a distant second at 28 per cent, followed by cyclists at 10 per cent. Walking and remote work were comparatively rare, accounting for just 5 and 2 per cent respectively.

By 2023, the picture had changed markedly. Car use almost halved to 30 per cent, placing it level with public transport, which rose slightly to the same figure. Cycling nearly doubled to 18 per cent, and walking edged up from 5 to 7 per cent. The most dramatic change, however, came in remote working, which jumped from 2 per cent in 2010 to 15 per cent in 2023 — a sevenfold rise. Taken together, these shifts point to a move away from private-car dependency towards greener and more flexible alternatives.
feature_hints: Car and public transport equal by 2023

title: Breakdown of household energy use in an average home, 2023
question_text: The pie chart below shows how household energy is used in an average home in an Asian country in 2023. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.
sample_band_7_answer:
The pie chart illustrates how energy is consumed in an average household in an Asian country in 2023, broken down into six categories.

Overall, controlling indoor temperature was by far the most energy-intensive activity in the home, while cooking consumed only a negligible share. The remaining categories contributed similar, moderate amounts.

Space heating and cooling accounted for the largest slice at 42 per cent — nearly half of total household consumption. Water heating came a distant second, using 18 per cent of energy. These two heat-related activities together made up three-fifths of all domestic energy use.

The remaining four categories each claimed smaller and relatively similar shares. Electronics and appliances consumed 13 per cent, closely followed by lighting at 12 per cent and refrigeration at 10 per cent. Cooking required only 5 per cent of the total — the smallest proportion of all. It is worth noting that if space heating, cooling and water heating are combined, they accounted for 60 per cent of energy usage, underlining the dominance of thermal needs in household consumption.

title: Distribution of global internet users by region, 2023
question_text: The pie chart below shows the distribution of the world's internet users by region in 2023. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.
sample_band_7_answer:
The pie chart depicts the regional distribution of internet users worldwide in 2023, divided into six regions.

Overall, Asia was by far the largest contributor to the global online population, accounting for more than half of all users, while the Middle East had the smallest share. The remaining four regions each held relatively modest proportions.

Asia dominated the global internet landscape with 52 per cent of all users — more than the other five regions combined. Europe came a distant second at 14 per cent, followed closely by Africa at 11 per cent and Latin America at 10 per cent. These three regions together represented roughly one-third of the world's online community.

North America and the Middle East occupied the smallest slices, with 7 and 6 per cent respectively. Given that North America has a relatively small population yet high internet penetration, its modest share reflects population size rather than limited access. Conversely, Africa's 11 per cent, drawn from a much larger population base, suggests considerably lower connectivity rates per person compared with the other regions.
feature_hints: North America modest 7% reflects penetration vs pop

title: Composition of household waste in a European country, 2022
question_text: The pie chart below shows the composition of household waste, by weight, in a European country in 2022. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.
sample_band_7_answer:
The pie chart shows the breakdown, by weight, of household waste produced in a European country during 2022, divided into six categories.

Overall, organic and paper waste together accounted for more than half of all refuse generated, while metal represented the smallest portion. Recyclable materials in general made up a substantial share of the total.

Food and organic waste formed the largest segment, making up 32 per cent of household refuse. Paper and cardboard followed at 22 per cent, meaning that these two categories alone contributed over 54 per cent of all waste. This suggests significant potential for composting and recycling schemes.

Plastic represented the third largest category at 16 per cent, a figure consistent with the widespread use of packaging in European households. Glass and metal contributed much less, at 10 and 6 per cent respectively. The miscellaneous category of 'other' waste — likely comprising textiles, electronics and hazardous materials — accounted for 14 per cent. Collectively, the three traditional recyclable streams (paper, plastic and glass) made up 48 per cent, indicating strong opportunities for material recovery.

title: Share of social media platform use among teenagers, 2023
question_text: The pie chart below shows the share of time teenagers aged 13-17 spent on different social media platforms in 2023. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.
sample_band_7_answer:
The pie chart illustrates how teenagers aged 13 to 17 distributed their social media time across six categories of platform in 2023.

Overall, TikTok was the clear leader among young users, capturing roughly one-third of total social media use. Facebook, once the most popular platform, had fallen to a marginal position, matched only by the miscellaneous 'other' category.

TikTok dominated the pie chart at 34 per cent, commanding more time than Instagram and YouTube combined in isolation but only slightly. Instagram came second at 22 per cent, very closely trailed by YouTube at 20 per cent. These two platforms, both owned by major technology companies, together accounted for 42 per cent of teenage social media time.

Snapchat represented a smaller but still meaningful slice at 12 per cent, reflecting its continued appeal among younger users for private messaging. Facebook, notably, held just 6 per cent — the same share as all 'other' platforms combined. The data clearly indicates a generational shift away from the older text-based networks towards short-form video platforms such as TikTok and Instagram Reels.

title: Modes of commuting in a medium-sized Vietnamese city, 2023
question_text: The pie chart below shows the modes of commuting used by residents of a medium-sized Vietnamese city in 2023. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.
sample_band_7_answer:
The pie chart illustrates the proportion of residents in a medium-sized Vietnamese city using six different modes of commuting in 2023.

Overall, motorcycles were the dominant form of daily transport, chosen by nearly three-fifths of all commuters. Private cars and public buses held similar, secondary positions, while non-motorised modes such as cycling and walking played a minor role.

Motorcycles clearly dominated the city's commuting landscape, with 58 per cent of residents relying on them — more than the combined share of all other modes. This reflects the vehicle's affordability and practicality for navigating dense urban streets.

Public bus use (14 per cent) narrowly exceeded the private car (12 per cent), suggesting that public transport remained a significant option despite growing private vehicle ownership. The two modes together accounted for just over a quarter of all trips. Bicycles represented 8 per cent of commuting, ahead of walking at 6 per cent, while 'other' modes — likely including ride-hailing and taxis — made up a marginal 2 per cent. These figures reveal a city where motorised two-wheelers remain deeply embedded in daily life.
feature_hints: Motorised two-wheelers central to urban life

title: Population of five major world cities (millions), 1990, 2005 and 2020
question_text: The table below shows the population, in millions, of five major world cities in 1990, 2005 and 2020. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.
sample_band_7_answer:
The table provides figures for the population, in millions, of five major world cities — Tokyo, Delhi, Shanghai, São Paulo and Cairo — in 1990, 2005 and 2020.

Overall, all five cities expanded between 1990 and 2020, but the rate of growth varied enormously. Tokyo remained the largest urban area throughout the period, while Delhi and Shanghai experienced particularly explosive growth and more than tripled in size.

In 1990, Tokyo was already by far the largest of the five cities, with 32.5 million inhabitants, well ahead of São Paulo at 14.8 million. Delhi, Shanghai and Cairo were comparatively small, ranging from 7.8 to 9.7 million. Over the following 30 years, however, the gap narrowed dramatically.

Delhi posted the most striking expansion, tripling from 9.7 to 30.3 million, while Shanghai's population grew from 7.8 to 27.1 million — a 3.5-fold increase. Cairo also more than doubled, reaching 20.9 million by 2020. São Paulo and Tokyo, by contrast, grew at a much slower pace, rising by roughly 50 per cent and 15 per cent respectively. As a result, Tokyo's lead, once substantial, shrank considerably by 2020.
feature_hints: Tokyo largest throughout but slowest growth

title: Adult literacy rate (%) in five countries, 1990, 2005 and 2020
question_text: The table below shows the adult literacy rate (percentage of adults aged 15 and over able to read and write) in five countries in 1990, 2005 and 2020. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.
sample_band_7_answer:
The table presents adult literacy rates, as a percentage of those aged 15 and above, for five Asian countries in 1990, 2005 and 2020.

Overall, literacy improved in every country over the three decades, but the magnitude of improvement differed sharply. Countries that started from a lower base — notably Bangladesh and India — achieved the most dramatic gains, while already-literate nations such as South Korea saw only marginal changes.

In 1990, South Korea led by a wide margin with a 96.3 per cent literacy rate, followed by Vietnam at 87.6 per cent and Indonesia at 82 per cent. India and Bangladesh lagged far behind, recording just 48.2 and 35.3 per cent respectively — meaning that over half of their adult populations could not read or write.

By 2020, the gap had narrowed considerably. South Korea climbed to near-universal literacy at 99.4 per cent, a rise of only 3.1 percentage points. Indonesia and Vietnam also advanced, reaching 96 and 95.8 per cent. The most remarkable progress, however, occurred in Bangladesh, whose rate more than doubled to 74.9 per cent, and India, which rose by nearly 30 percentage points to 77.7 per cent.

title: Average monthly income (USD) by occupation in a Southeast Asian country, 2015 and 2022
question_text: The table below shows the average monthly income, in US dollars, across five occupational categories in a Southeast Asian country in 2015 and 2022. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.
sample_band_7_answer:
The table compares the average monthly income, in US dollars, of five occupational groups in a Southeast Asian country between 2015 and 2022.

Overall, wages rose in every occupation during the seven-year period, but the gains were distributed unequally: white-collar and knowledge-based professions enjoyed far larger percentage increases than manual or agricultural work, which widened the income gap.

IT professionals saw the sharpest rise, with their monthly earnings nearly doubling from 850 to 1,620 dollars — an increase of almost 91 per cent. Doctors followed in second place: their wages climbed by 64 per cent, from 720 to 1,180 dollars, keeping them the second best-paid group. Teachers also saw meaningful progress, with earnings rising by roughly 48 per cent to 710 dollars.

At the other end of the scale, manual workers and farmers experienced much slower growth. Factory workers' wages grew by 35.5 per cent, from 310 to 420 dollars, while farmers registered the smallest gain in both absolute and percentage terms, moving from 180 to 240 dollars. As a result, the ratio between the highest and lowest paid occupations widened from 4.7 times in 2015 to 6.75 times by 2022.

title: World university rankings: selected universities by region, 2023
question_text: The table below shows data for five leading universities from different regions in 2023, including their ranking, number of students, proportion of international students, and faculty-to-student ratio. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.
sample_band_7_answer:
The table provides data on five leading universities from different regions in 2023, comparing their global ranking, student numbers, international-student proportion and faculty-to-student ratio.

Overall, US and UK institutions occupied the top ranks but had the most favourable staffing conditions, while the two larger universities in Asia and Australia ranked considerably lower and hosted far more students per staff member.

Harvard topped the global list at number one, with a comparatively modest student body of 23,700 and the best faculty ratio of one staff member to every six students. Oxford, at fourth place, enrolled 26,400 students and had a ratio of 1 to 11. Despite being smaller than Melbourne, these two universities stood out for their academic prestige.

Melbourne, in contrast, was the largest by enrolment at 52,400 but ranked 33rd globally, with the highest faculty-student ratio at 1:19. It also drew the greatest proportion of international students, at 45 per cent, closely followed by Oxford at 42 per cent. By contrast, the University of Tokyo ranked 28th and had the lowest international share, at just 15 per cent. NUS Singapore fell in the middle at rank 19 with balanced figures.

title: Installed renewable energy capacity (gigawatts) in four countries, 2012, 2017 and 2022
question_text: The table below shows the installed capacity of three renewable energy sources — solar, wind and hydroelectric — in four countries in 2012, 2017 and 2022, measured in gigawatts. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.
sample_band_7_answer:
The table presents installed solar and wind capacity, in gigawatts, across four countries — China, the United States, Germany and India — in 2012, 2017 and 2022.

Overall, renewable capacity expanded in every country over the decade, but China stood out for the sheer scale of its deployment, particularly in solar energy. Solar capacity also grew more rapidly than wind in most cases, notably in countries that started from a low base.

China experienced the most dramatic transformation. Its solar capacity surged nearly fiftyfold, from just 8 GW in 2012 to 393 GW in 2022, while its wind capacity rose sixfold to 365 GW. By 2022, China alone operated roughly three-quarters of all solar and wind capacity shown in the table.

The United States followed a similar but far more modest trajectory: solar grew from 8 GW to 113 GW, while wind reached 142 GW. India started from the smallest base but expanded its solar capacity from a mere 1 GW to 63 GW — a 63-fold increase. Germany's growth was gentler, with solar and wind both reaching 66 GW by 2022. Notably, solar overtook wind in three of the four countries, whereas a decade earlier wind had dominated everywhere except Germany.
feature_hints: Solar overtook wind in most countries by 2022
China's solar surged fiftyfold (8 to 393 GW)

title: Unemployment rates in four countries, 2010-2022
question_text: The line chart below shows the unemployment rate, as a percentage of the labour force, in Spain, France, Germany and Japan from 2010 to 2022. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.
sample_band_7_answer:
The line chart illustrates unemployment rates, expressed as a percentage of the labour force, in four countries — Spain, France, Germany and Japan — between 2010 and 2022.

Overall, Spain consistently recorded the highest unemployment figures, while Japan and Germany maintained the lowest rates throughout. All four countries saw their unemployment decline towards the end of the period, despite a temporary rise in 2020.

Spain began with a strikingly high rate of 19.9 per cent in 2010, which climbed further to a peak of 26.1 per cent in 2013, at the height of the eurozone crisis. It then fell steadily, reaching 14.1 per cent in 2019 before easing to 12.9 per cent in 2022 — still more than four times Germany's level. France followed a gentler pattern, hovering around 9 to 10 per cent from 2010 to 2016 before declining to 7.3 per cent by the end of the period.

Germany and Japan, in contrast, enjoyed a consistent downward trend. German unemployment halved from 7 per cent in 2010 to 3.1 per cent in 2022, while Japan's rate dropped from 5.1 per cent to a mere 2.6 per cent. The 2020 pandemic caused only modest spikes in all four countries before rates resumed their decline.

title: Adult obesity rates in three age groups, 1995-2020
question_text: The line chart below shows the percentage of adults classified as obese in three age groups in a developed country from 1995 to 2020. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.
sample_band_7_answer:
The line chart illustrates the percentage of adults classified as obese in three age groups — 20 to 39, 40 to 59, and 60 and over — in a developed country between 1995 and 2020.

Overall, obesity rates rose substantially across all three age groups during the 25-year period. The middle-aged group consistently had the highest figures, while the youngest adults remained the leanest, though the gap narrowed over time.

In 1995, the 40-to-59 group had the highest obesity rate at 20 per cent, slightly above the 60-plus group at 18 per cent. The youngest adults aged 20 to 39 had the lowest rate, at 14 per cent. All three groups then climbed sharply over the following ten years, with figures reaching 22, 28 and 26 per cent respectively by 2005.

Between 2005 and 2020, obesity continued to rise in every age group. The middle-aged category reached 40 per cent by 2020 — double its 1995 level. Those over 60 followed a similar pattern, rising to 36 per cent, while young adults saw the most dramatic relative change, more than doubling from 14 to 32 per cent. By 2020, the gap between the highest and lowest rates had narrowed to just 8 percentage points.

title: Share of electricity generated from renewable sources in three countries, 2005-2022
question_text: The line chart below shows the percentage of electricity generated from renewable sources (solar, wind and hydro) in Denmark, the United Kingdom and Australia from 2005 to 2022. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.
sample_band_7_answer:
The line chart depicts the share of electricity generated from renewable sources in Denmark, the United Kingdom and Australia over a 17-year period from 2005 to 2022.

Overall, all three countries substantially increased their renewable electricity share during the period, but the pace and scale of transition differed considerably. Denmark stood out as the clear leader, whereas Australia lagged furthest behind despite notable progress.

Denmark began the period with a relatively high starting point of 27 per cent — well above its two counterparts. Its renewable share then climbed at an accelerating rate, surpassing the symbolic 50-per-cent threshold by 2015 and reaching an impressive 82 per cent by 2022. The United Kingdom, which generated a mere 4 per cent from renewables in 2005, experienced the most dramatic relative transformation: its share leapt more than tenfold to 43 per cent by 2020, before dipping slightly to 41 per cent in 2022.

Australia recorded the slowest trajectory. From 8 per cent in 2005, its renewable share rose only gradually to 14 per cent by 2015 before gaining momentum, reaching 32 per cent in 2022. By the end of the period, Denmark's share was more than 2.5 times that of Australia.

title: Sales of physical books and e-books in the United States, 2012-2022
question_text: The line chart below shows annual sales of physical books and e-books in the United States, in millions of units, from 2012 to 2022. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.
sample_band_7_answer:
The line chart illustrates annual sales of physical books and e-books, measured in millions of units, in the United States between 2012 and 2022.

Overall, physical book sales grew steadily throughout the decade, while e-book sales followed a far less stable trajectory. Contrary to predictions of a digital takeover, printed books remained significantly more popular at the end of the period than at the beginning.

In 2012, physical books dominated the market, with 612 million units sold — roughly twice the 310 million e-books sold that year. Over the following ten years, print sales climbed steadily, passing 675 million in 2016 and reaching a peak of 788 million in 2022, an increase of approximately 29 per cent overall.

E-book sales, by contrast, displayed a far more erratic pattern. From their 2012 level, they fell sharply to just 196 million in 2016, losing more than a third of their volume. A modest recovery followed, with sales climbing to 246 million during the 2020 pandemic year, when people spent more time indoors. However, this uptick proved short-lived: by 2022, e-book sales had slipped again to 202 million, leaving the format well below its 2012 starting point.
feature_hints: Physical books grew steadily (612 to 788 million)

title: Average house prices in three major cities, 2000-2022
question_text: The line chart below shows the average house price (in thousands of US dollars, inflation-adjusted) in three major cities from 2000 to 2022. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.
sample_band_7_answer:
The line chart presents inflation-adjusted average house prices, in thousands of US dollars, in Sydney, Toronto and Berlin between 2000 and 2022.

Overall, property prices rose in all three cities over the 22-year period, but the scale of appreciation varied markedly. Sydney consistently commanded the highest prices, while Berlin followed a much more subdued trajectory despite a sharp acceleration in the final decade.

In 2000, Sydney homes averaged 340 thousand dollars, well above Toronto at 280 thousand and Berlin at 210 thousand. Sydney's prices rose almost continuously, reaching 720 thousand by 2015 and surging to 1.05 million by 2022 — an increase of more than threefold. Toronto followed a similar but less steep path, climbing from 280 thousand to 940 thousand over the same period.

Berlin, by contrast, remained relatively flat until 2010, even dipping to 195 thousand in 2005 as the market stagnated. From 2010 onwards, however, prices accelerated sharply, doubling to 430 thousand by 2020 and reaching 520 thousand in 2022. Nevertheless, Berlin homes at the end of the period cost only around half as much as those in Sydney, underlining the city's relative affordability despite rapid recent gains.
feature_hints: Toronto followed Sydney pattern more moderately

title: Number of registered users on three online learning platforms, 2017-2023
question_text: The line chart below shows the number of registered users, in millions, on three online learning platforms between 2017 and 2023. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.
sample_band_7_answer:
The line chart illustrates the number of registered users, in millions, on three online learning platforms — A, B and C — from 2017 to 2023.

Overall, user numbers grew substantially on all three platforms over the seven-year period, with particularly steep increases in 2020. Platform A remained the clear market leader throughout, while Platform C expanded at the fastest relative pace.

In 2017, Platform A had 24 million users, twice as many as Platform B (12 million) and almost five times more than Platform C, which started from a small base of just 5 million. Between 2017 and 2019, all three platforms saw steady growth, with Platform A reaching 40 million, Platform B 28 million and Platform C 14 million.

The most dramatic expansion, however, occurred in 2020, when all three platforms nearly doubled their user bases during the global pandemic. Platform A jumped to 77 million, Platform B to 60 million and Platform C to 41 million. Growth continued after 2020, although at a slower pace. By 2023, Platform A had reached 105 million registered users, Platform B stood at 82 million, and Platform C — starting smallest — had multiplied nearly thirteenfold to 64 million.

title: Number of large companies with net-zero commitments by region, 2015-2023
question_text: The line chart below shows the number of large companies that had publicly committed to net-zero carbon emissions by region between 2015 and 2023. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.
sample_band_7_answer:
The line chart illustrates the number of large companies that had publicly pledged to achieve net-zero carbon emissions across four global regions between 2015 and 2023.

Overall, corporate net-zero commitments accelerated dramatically in every region over the eight-year period, with particularly explosive growth after 2020. Europe consistently led the way, while the Rest of the World recorded the smallest, though still substantial, rise.

At the start of the period, net-zero commitments were extremely rare. Europe led with just 42 companies in 2015, followed by North America at 18, Asia at 8 and the Rest of the World at a mere 4. All four regions recorded modest growth through 2018, but figures remained below 200. The picture changed radically from 2020 onwards.

Between 2020 and 2023, Europe's total surged from 520 to 1,480 companies, almost tripling. North America followed a parallel path, climbing from 290 to 1,050, while Asia expanded most rapidly in relative terms, rising from 140 to 760 — a more than fivefold jump. The Rest of the World, though still trailing, also expanded considerably, reaching 420 companies by 2023. The combined total rose from 72 companies in 2015 to approximately 3,710 by 2023.
feature_hints: Rest of World smallest but still grew 100x

title: Popular leisure activities by age group in a country, 2023
question_text: The bar chart below shows the percentage of adults in three age groups who regularly participate in four leisure activities in a country in 2023. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.
sample_band_7_answer:
The bar chart compares the percentage of adults in three age groups — 18 to 34, 35 to 54, and 55 and over — who regularly take part in four leisure activities in a country in 2023.

Overall, watching streaming services was the most common activity across all age categories, while reading books was the only activity in which the oldest group led. Young adults were the most active in three of the four pursuits.

Streaming was particularly popular among 18-to-34-year-olds, with 92 per cent watching regularly, compared with 78 per cent of middle-aged adults and 62 per cent of those aged 55 and over. A similar pattern emerged for exercising: 68 per cent of young adults exercised regularly, against just 40 per cent of the oldest group.

Socialising outdoors showed the widest generational gap, ranging from 72 per cent among young adults down to 44 per cent among seniors. The only exception to this trend was reading, in which 66 per cent of those aged 55 and over read regularly — significantly higher than either the 35-to-54 group (42 per cent) or the youngest adults (just 38 per cent). As a result, reading was the only pursuit where participation increased with age.
feature_hints: Youngest most active in 3 of 4 activities
Reading only activity rising with age

title: Reasons students chose a particular university, 2015 and 2023
question_text: The bar chart below shows the percentage of university applicants who cited six different reasons for choosing their university in 2015 and 2023. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.
sample_band_7_answer:
The bar chart compares the percentage of university applicants citing six different reasons for choosing their university in 2015 and 2023.

Overall, practical and financial considerations became markedly more important over the eight-year period, while the weight given to academic reputation and campus location fell noticeably. Graduate employability emerged as the single leading factor by 2023.

In 2015, academic reputation was the most frequently cited reason, mentioned by 62 per cent of applicants, followed by course content (58 per cent) and campus location (55 per cent). Career prospects and graduate employability were mentioned by roughly half and 40 per cent of students respectively, while only 38 per cent cited tuition cost.

By 2023, the ranking had shifted substantially. Graduate employability topped the list at 70 per cent — a 30-percentage-point rise — closely followed by career prospects at 68 per cent and tuition cost at 65 per cent. Tuition cost therefore saw the largest percentage-point increase, reflecting growing financial pressure on students. Course content remained stable, while academic reputation slipped to 54 per cent and campus location dropped from 55 to 42 per cent, the steepest fall in the chart. These shifts suggest a clear move towards outcome- and cost-driven decision-making.
feature_hints: Graduate employability emerged as top factor (70%)

title: Average starting salary by degree level in four fields, 2023
question_text: The bar chart below shows the average starting salary (in thousand US dollars per year) of graduates in four fields, by degree level, in 2023. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.
sample_band_7_answer:
The bar chart illustrates average starting salaries, in thousands of US dollars, for graduates in four fields at three degree levels — Bachelor's, Master's and PhD — in 2023.

Overall, computer science graduates commanded the highest starting salaries across every level of qualification, while humanities graduates earned the least. Salaries rose substantially with each additional degree, but the returns varied noticeably by field.

At Bachelor's level, computer science led with 78 thousand dollars, ahead of engineering (72 thousand) and business (62 thousand). Humanities graduates earned considerably less, at just 45 thousand dollars. The ranking remained unchanged at Master's level: computer science rose to 98 thousand, engineering to 88 thousand and business to 82 thousand, whereas humanities reached only 54 thousand.

PhD holders enjoyed the highest earnings in each field. Computer science PhDs commanded 125 thousand dollars — 60 per cent more than Bachelor's holders in the same field — while engineering and business PhDs earned 108 and 105 thousand dollars respectively. Humanities PhDs, at 62 thousand dollars, received the smallest absolute and relative boost. The gap between computer science and humanities grew from 33 thousand at Bachelor's level to 63 thousand at PhD level, highlighting widening disparities at higher qualification levels.
feature_hints: Computer Science highest at every level
Salary rises with degree level in all fields
Humanities smallest absolute boost from PhD

title: Annual retail sales of organic food by category in a European country, 2013 and 2023
question_text: The bar chart below shows annual retail sales of organic food, in million US dollars, across five product categories in a European country in 2013 and 2023. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.
sample_band_7_answer:
The bar chart illustrates annual retail sales of organic food, in millions of US dollars, across five product categories in a European country in 2013 and 2023.

Overall, organic food sales expanded considerably in every category during the decade, with the market more than doubling in most segments. Fruit and vegetables remained the dominant category, while beverages grew the fastest in relative terms.

In 2013, fruit and vegetables accounted for the largest share of organic sales at 420 million dollars, well ahead of dairy at 280 million. Bread and cereals and meat contributed 180 and 150 million dollars respectively, while beverages trailed as the smallest category at just 90 million. Total organic sales in 2013 amounted to roughly 1.12 billion dollars.

By 2023, the market had expanded substantially. Fruit and vegetables reached 920 million dollars, more than doubling its 2013 level and reinforcing its dominance. Dairy rose to 540 million, while meat sales nearly doubled to 260 million. Bread and cereals almost doubled to 350 million. The most striking growth, however, occurred in organic beverages, which climbed from 90 to 240 million dollars — a 167-per-cent increase. Collectively, the total organic market almost doubled over the decade, reaching 2.31 billion dollars.
feature_hints: Meat smallest starting and ending category

title: Water usage by sector in three countries, 2022
question_text: The bar chart below shows water consumption by sector — agriculture, industry and domestic use — in three countries in 2022. Figures are given as a percentage of each country's total water usage. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.
sample_band_7_answer:
The bar chart compares the share of water usage by sector in India, the United States and Germany in 2022, with figures expressed as a percentage of each country's total consumption.

Overall, the distribution of water use varied enormously by country, reflecting different stages of economic development. Agriculture dominated in India, industry was by far the biggest user in Germany, while the United States showed a more balanced pattern.

In India, agriculture accounted for an overwhelming 78 per cent of water usage, with industry and domestic use trailing at 12 and 10 per cent respectively. This pattern is consistent with an agriculture-heavy economy in which a large rural workforce depends on irrigation.

Germany presented the reverse picture: a mere 2 per cent of its water was used in agriculture, while industry consumed a dominant 85 per cent — the highest figure in the chart. Domestic consumption in Germany stood at 13 per cent, roughly in line with the other two countries.

The United States displayed the most balanced profile. Industry claimed the largest share at 46 per cent, followed closely by agriculture at 40 per cent, while domestic use accounted for 14 per cent. Domestic consumption, interestingly, varied very little between the three nations, ranging from just 10 to 14 per cent.
feature_hints: US most balanced between agriculture and industry
Domestic use similar across all three (10-14%)

title: Health expenditure per capita and as percentage of GDP in five countries, 2022
question_text: The bar chart below shows health expenditure per capita (in US dollars) and as a percentage of GDP in five countries in 2022. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.
sample_band_7_answer:
The bar chart compares health expenditure per capita, measured in US dollars, with health spending as a share of GDP across five countries in 2022.

Overall, the United States stood out on both metrics, spending dramatically more than the other nations in absolute terms and also dedicating the highest proportion of its economy to healthcare. Mexico ranked lowest on both measures.

American per-capita health spending reached 12,550 dollars — more than 50 per cent higher than Germany's figure of 8,010 dollars, and over ten times Mexico's 1,160 dollars. The United Kingdom and Japan occupied middle positions at 5,390 and 4,590 dollars respectively. These differences in absolute spending largely reflected each country's overall wealth.

The share of GDP devoted to healthcare followed a similar but less extreme pattern. The United States again led with 17.8 per cent, well above Germany's 12.7 per cent. Japan and the United Kingdom spent remarkably similar proportions at 11.5 and 11.3 per cent, while Mexico dedicated just 6.2 per cent of its GDP to health. Notably, although Japan's per-capita spending was lower than the UK's, it devoted a slightly higher share of GDP to health, suggesting a relatively strong commitment to healthcare given its economic size.
feature_hints: Japan lower per capita but higher GDP share than UK
UK and Japan close on GDP share

title: Reported crime rates per 100,000 people by category in three cities, 2023
question_text: The bar chart below shows reported crime rates per 100,000 people across four crime categories in three cities in 2023. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.
sample_band_7_answer:
The bar chart shows reported crime rates per 100,000 inhabitants across four categories — burglary, assault, theft, and fraud/cybercrime — in three cities in 2023.

Overall, theft was by far the most common offence in all three cities, whereas the pattern for other crime types varied considerably. City C recorded the highest rates of property-related crime, while City B stood out for fraud and personal violence.

Theft dominated the crime landscape everywhere. City C topped the list with 3,420 thefts per 100,000 people, well ahead of City A (2,340) and City B (1,860). A similar ranking was observed for burglary: City C again led with 1,220 reported cases, more than three times City B's 410 and noticeably above City A's 890.

The two other categories, however, reversed this pattern. City B recorded the most assaults, at 720 per 100,000 inhabitants — nearly double City C's 380 — and also the highest rate of fraud and cybercrime at 1,150, almost 70 per cent higher than City A's 680 and more than double City C's 520. These contrasts suggest that City C struggles most with property-based offences, while City B shows higher levels of both interpersonal violence and digital crime.
feature_hints: Theft dominates in all three cities
City C highest for theft and burglary
City B highest for assault and fraud/cybercrime
City A middle-ranked across most categories

title: Main causes of global deforestation, 2022
question_text: The pie chart below shows the main causes of deforestation worldwide in 2022. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.
sample_band_7_answer:
The pie chart shows the main causes of global deforestation in 2022, broken down into six categories.

Overall, agricultural activities accounted for the lion's share of forest loss, with cattle ranching alone being responsible for more than any other cause. Non-agricultural drivers such as logging and infrastructure played a comparatively minor role.

Cattle ranching was by far the largest contributor to deforestation, responsible for 41 per cent of forest clearance worldwide. Oilseed crops — particularly soy and palm oil — came in second place at 18 per cent, while smallholder agriculture accounted for 14 per cent. Taken together, these three agriculture-related causes made up 73 per cent of all deforestation, underlining the dominant role that food production plays in forest loss.

Logging for timber contributed a further 12 per cent, while infrastructure development and urban expansion accounted for 9 per cent. The miscellaneous 'other' category — which includes mining, fires and natural causes — represented the smallest share at 6 per cent. The data therefore suggests that meaningful reductions in deforestation would require major changes in the global agricultural system, especially in how meat and plant-based oils are produced.

title: Breakdown of central government budget in an OECD country, 2023
question_text: The pie chart below shows the breakdown of central government expenditure in an OECD country in 2023. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.
sample_band_7_answer:
The pie chart illustrates how the central government of an OECD country allocated its expenditure in 2023, divided into seven spending categories.

Overall, social and welfare-related spending dominated the budget, with pensions and healthcare alone accounting for more than half of total outlay. Traditional state functions such as defence and infrastructure represented comparatively small shares.

Social protection and pensions formed the single largest item at 34 per cent — more than one-third of total spending. Healthcare came in second at 19 per cent, meaning that the two largest welfare-related categories together consumed 53 per cent of the budget. This heavy weighting reflects the demographic and healthcare pressures typical of advanced economies.

Education represented 12 per cent of expenditure, while 'other public services' — encompassing items such as police, justice and general administration — absorbed 11 per cent. Defence and debt interest accounted for 9 and 8 per cent respectively, illustrating the substantial burden that servicing public debt now places on national budgets. Public infrastructure received the smallest identified share at just 7 per cent, despite its importance to long-term economic growth. Collectively, non-welfare spending amounted to 47 per cent, slightly less than the combined welfare categories.

title: Daily calorie intake by food group for an average adult, 2023
question_text: The pie chart below shows the composition of daily calorie intake by food group for an average adult in a country in 2023. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.
sample_band_7_answer:
The pie chart illustrates the composition of daily calorie intake by food group for an average adult in a country in 2023, divided into six categories.

Overall, grains and animal products accounted for more than half of all calories consumed, while fruits and vegetables and oils provided the smallest shares. The data suggests a diet that leans towards starches and proteins rather than fresh produce.

Grains and cereals represented the single largest source of calories at 32 per cent, highlighting the continued role of foods such as bread, rice and pasta as the basis of the average diet. Meat and poultry followed in second place at 22 per cent, while dairy products contributed a further 15 per cent. Together these three categories supplied 69 per cent of daily calories.

Fruits and vegetables accounted for just 14 per cent of intake — a surprisingly low figure given public-health recommendations. Added sugars and sweets, often targeted by nutritionists, contributed 10 per cent of calories, while oils and fats provided the smallest share at 7 per cent. The contribution of added sugars therefore exceeded that of dietary fats, and the entire discretionary-food category (sugars plus oils) amounted to 17 per cent — higher than the share from fruit and vegetables.

title: Global electricity generation by source, 2022
question_text: The pie chart below shows the share of global electricity generation by energy source in 2022. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.
sample_band_7_answer:
The pie chart illustrates the share of global electricity generation by energy source in 2022, divided into seven categories.

Overall, fossil fuels remained the dominant source of electricity worldwide, together accounting for almost two-thirds of total generation. Renewable sources, although growing, still supplied less than a third of global electricity.

Coal continued to be the single largest source of electricity, generating 36 per cent of the global total — more than any other fuel. Natural gas came in second at 22 per cent, meaning that fossil fuels combined (with oil and other fuels adding a further 5 per cent) supplied 63 per cent of all electricity. This heavy reliance on fossil fuels highlights the scale of the transition still required to meet climate targets.

Low-carbon sources together contributed 37 per cent of global generation. Hydroelectric power was the most important of these, providing 15 per cent of electricity, followed by nuclear at 10 per cent. Wind and solar, despite their rapid growth in recent years, still accounted for modest shares of 7 and 5 per cent respectively. Collectively, wind and solar — often considered the most scalable renewable technologies — supplied just 12 per cent of the world's electricity in 2022.

title: Reasons cited by adults for doing regular exercise, 2023
question_text: The pie chart below shows the reasons cited by adults in a country for doing regular exercise in 2023. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.
sample_band_7_answer:
The pie chart shows the reasons cited by adults in a country for engaging in regular exercise in 2023, divided into six categories.

Overall, health-related motivations dominated the responses, with general physical health being cited far more often than any other reason. Social and competitive motivations played a much smaller role in people's decisions to exercise.

Maintaining physical health was the most common reason given by adults, accounting for 38 per cent of responses. Weight management came in second at 22 per cent, followed by mental health and stress relief at 18 per cent. These three health-related categories together made up 78 per cent of all reasons — a clear indication that people chose exercise primarily for its health benefits.

Social motivations were less prominent. Exercising as a way to spend time with friends was cited by just 10 per cent of respondents, while 7 per cent exercised mainly to improve their performance in a particular sport. The remaining 5 per cent reported other reasons, such as medical advice or hobby-related activity. Notably, despite the growing public attention given to mental wellbeing, it still ranked behind physical health and weight control as a driver of exercise.
feature_hints: Social and sport reasons minor (10% and 7%)

title: Foreign direct investment (FDI) inflows by sector in a developing country, 2010, 2017 and 2022 (billion USD)
question_text: The table below shows foreign direct investment inflows, in billions of US dollars, into five sectors of a developing country's economy in 2010, 2017 and 2022. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.
sample_band_7_answer:
The table presents foreign direct investment inflows, measured in billions of US dollars, into five sectors of a developing country's economy in 2010, 2017 and 2022.

Overall, total FDI expanded substantially over the 12-year period, but inflows into different sectors followed sharply diverging paths. Manufacturing and technology emerged as the two main magnets of foreign capital by 2022, whereas investment in the energy and mining sector contracted.

In 2010, manufacturing attracted the largest FDI inflow at 4.2 billion dollars, closely followed by energy and mining at 3.1 billion and real estate at 2.8 billion. Technology attracted a negligible 0.3 billion, reflecting the country's then-limited digital economy. Finance and banking accounted for 1.5 billion.

By 2022, the landscape had changed dramatically. Manufacturing FDI had more than doubled to 9.5 billion dollars, reinforcing its dominance. The technology sector showed the most striking transformation, surging from 0.3 to 7.4 billion — a 24-fold increase that made it the second-largest recipient of FDI. In contrast, real estate investment, having peaked at 5.1 billion in 2017, declined to 3.9 billion by 2022, while energy and mining shrank continuously from 3.1 to 1.8 billion. Finance recorded modest but steady growth.
feature_hints: Manufacturing largest throughout and more than doubled
Real estate peaked in 2017 then fell

title: Annual average air quality index (AQI) in five Asian cities, 2015 and 2022
question_text: The table below shows annual average air quality index (AQI) values and the number of days per year with hazardous air quality in five Asian cities in 2015 and 2022. Higher AQI values indicate worse air quality. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.
sample_band_7_answer:
The table compares annual average air quality index (AQI) values and the number of days rated as hazardous in five Asian cities in 2015 and 2022. Higher AQI values indicate worse air quality.

Overall, air quality improved substantially in Delhi and Beijing, while it deteriorated in Jakarta and Hanoi. Tokyo remained exceptionally clean throughout, with no hazardous days in either year.

Delhi recorded the poorest air quality in both years, but its AQI improved from 168 to 142, and the number of hazardous days almost halved from 108 to 72. Beijing registered the most dramatic progress: its average AQI almost halved from 145 to 82, and the number of hazardous days plunged from 96 in 2015 to just 14 in 2022, reflecting major policy efforts to curb emissions.

In contrast, Jakarta and Hanoi both saw air quality worsen. Jakarta's AQI rose from 96 to 112, and its hazardous days increased from 28 to 46. Hanoi followed a similar pattern, with its AQI climbing from 88 to 102 and hazardous days rising from 22 to 38 — in line with rapid urbanisation. Tokyo, with an AQI below 45 in both years and zero hazardous days, was by far the cleanest of the five cities.
feature_hints: Delhi worst in both years but improved

title: International tourist arrivals and average spending per visitor, five countries, 2022
question_text: The table below shows the number of international tourist arrivals (millions) and the average spending per visitor (in US dollars) in five countries in 2022, as well as total tourism revenue. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.
sample_band_7_answer:
The table provides data on international tourism in five countries in 2022, including the number of arrivals in millions, the average spending per visitor in US dollars and the resulting total revenue in billions of dollars.

Overall, Spain generated the highest tourism revenue, driven by a combination of high visitor numbers and relatively high spending per person. Thailand, despite receiving far fewer tourists, recorded the highest average spend per visitor, while Turkey attracted many visitors but earned comparatively little per head.

Spain led the table on both arrivals and total revenue, welcoming 71.6 million visitors and earning 88.8 billion dollars, with each tourist spending 1,240 dollars on average. France came second in both arrivals (66.6 million) and revenue (67.9 billion), even though the average spend per visitor was the lowest but one, at 1,020 dollars. Italy received 49.8 million tourists and earned 55.3 billion, with a mid-range average spend of 1,110 dollars.

Turkey presented an interesting contrast: although it welcomed 50.5 million tourists — more than Italy — its total revenue of 34.3 billion was the lowest except for Thailand, owing to an average spend of just 680 dollars. Thailand attracted the fewest visitors at 11.2 million, yet each tourist spent the most (1,340 dollars).
feature_hints: Thailand fewest arrivals but highest per-visitor spend
Turkey many arrivals but lowest per-visitor spend
Revenue driven by arrivals × per-visitor spend

title: Average crop yields (tonnes per hectare) in four countries, 2000, 2010 and 2020
question_text: The table below shows average yields of three major crops — rice, wheat and maize — in four countries in 2000, 2010 and 2020. Yields are measured in tonnes per hectare. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.
sample_band_7_answer:
The table provides average yields of rice, wheat and maize in four countries — China, India, the USA and Brazil — in 2000, 2010 and 2020, measured in tonnes per hectare.

Overall, crop yields improved in every case over the two decades, reflecting advances in agricultural technology and practices. However, absolute levels varied widely between countries, with the United States consistently producing the most per hectare.

The United States recorded the highest yields in the table, with maize production climbing from 8.6 tonnes per hectare in 2000 to 10.8 tonnes by 2020 — a rise of around 26 per cent. Brazilian maize farmers followed a steeper growth path: although they started at a much lower base of 3 tonnes, yields almost doubled to 5.8 tonnes over the same period.

Rice and wheat yields also improved in both China and India. Chinese rice yields climbed from 6.2 to 7 tonnes per hectare, while Chinese wheat yields rose more sharply from 3.8 to 5.7 tonnes. India, starting from a lower base, saw rice yields grow from 3 to 4.1 tonnes and wheat yields from 2.7 to 3.5 tonnes. Despite this progress, Indian yields in 2020 remained substantially lower than China's, suggesting scope for further productivity gains.
feature_hints: China outperforms India on both rice and wheat

title: Selected healthcare outcomes in four countries, 2022
question_text: The table below shows four healthcare outcome indicators in four countries in 2022. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant.
sample_band_7_answer:
The table compares four healthcare outcome indicators — life expectancy, infant mortality, doctors per 1,000 people and hospital beds per 1,000 people — across Japan, Norway, the United States and South Africa in 2022.

Overall, Japan and Norway recorded the strongest health outcomes, while South Africa lagged behind by a wide margin. The United States, despite being a high-income country, produced noticeably weaker outcomes than the other two developed nations.

Japan led on life expectancy at 84.3 years, narrowly ahead of Norway at 83.2. Americans lived an average of 76.1 years — nearly eight years less than the Japanese — while South Africans had the lowest life expectancy at only 62.3 years. Infant mortality followed a similar pattern: Japan and Norway recorded extremely low figures of 1.8 and 2 deaths per 1,000 births respectively, whereas South Africa's rate was 27.5 — more than 15 times higher.

The resources dedicated to healthcare varied considerably. Norway had by far the most doctors per capita at 5 per 1,000, while South Africa had just 0.8 — the lowest. Interestingly, Japan reported an extraordinary 13 hospital beds per 1,000 people, around four to five times the levels in Norway, the US and South Africa, suggesting a healthcare system oriented strongly towards inpatient care.
feature_hints: South Africa weakest across all four measures
US weaker than other developed nations
SA infant mortality 15x higher than Japan

question_text: Some people believe that universities should only offer places to students who achieve the highest grades in school-leaving exams. Others argue that universities should accept students from a wider range of backgrounds, even if their grades are lower. To what extent do you agree or disagree?
sample_band_7_answer:
The question of whether university admission should depend purely on exam performance has long been a contentious issue. While some argue that the highest scorers deserve the limited places available, I partly disagree and believe a wider intake delivers greater benefits.

Those in favour of grades-only admission often point to fairness and efficiency. Entrance examinations provide a measurable, objective standard that treats every candidate equally, regardless of social connections or personal circumstances. Universities, especially prestigious ones, also argue that high-achieving students are more likely to cope with demanding courses, thereby improving graduation rates and preserving academic reputation.

However, this narrow approach has clear limitations. Exam results reflect not only ability but also the quality of prior schooling, access to private tutoring, and home support — advantages unevenly distributed across society. A talented student from a rural area may therefore score lower than a less capable but better-prepared peer from a major city. Restricting admission to top scorers risks reinforcing existing inequalities and depriving universities of diverse perspectives that enrich discussion and research.

In my view, universities should use exam results as one important criterion rather than the only one.
