Parents play a central role too. Keeping books at home, reading aloud to younger children and visiting libraries together all build early habits. Public libraries can run reading challenges and author events that make books feel exciting rather than obligatory. Finally, governments can fund well-equipped school libraries and subsidise e-books, so that cost is not a barrier.

In conclusion, young people read less mainly because digital entertainment is faster and schools sometimes kill enjoyment. Renewing the love of reading requires better book choices, family habits and lively library culture.
key_ideas: Cause 1: smartphones and streaming offer easy entertainment
Cause 2: heavy academic pressure leaves no free time
Cause 3: parents don't model reading at home
Cause 4: school lists feature uninteresting classics
Solution 1: age-appropriate reading lists and graphic novels
Solution 2: daily silent reading and book clubs
Solution 3: parents reading with children
Solution 4: well-funded libraries and affordable e-books

question_text: Cybercrime, including online fraud and identity theft, has risen rapidly in recent years. What are the main causes, and what can individuals and governments do to respond?
sample_band_7_answer:
Cybercrime has become one of the fastest-growing categories of criminal activity in the world, costing individuals and businesses hundreds of billions of dollars each year. Understanding why it has expanded so rapidly, and what effective responses look like, is essential for governments, companies and ordinary users alike.

Several factors have fuelled the rise in cybercrime. The first is the simple fact that more of daily life has moved online — from banking and shopping to healthcare and social contact — giving criminals many more targets. Second, artificial intelligence and automated tools now allow criminals to launch sophisticated scams, such as deepfake voice calls or highly personalised phishing emails, at very low cost. Third, many victims still use weak passwords, reuse the same password across sites, and fail to enable two-factor authentication, making them easy to exploit. Finally, cross-border investigation is extremely difficult: criminals in one country can attack victims in another, often beyond the reach of local police.

The response must work at several levels. Individuals should adopt basic digital hygiene: use unique strong passwords stored in a password manager, activate two-factor authentication, and be sceptical of urgent requests for money or personal information, even when they appear to come from known contacts. Schools should teach digital safety from an early age, just as they teach road safety.

Governments must also act. They should fund specialised cybercrime police units, update laws to keep pace with new offences such as cryptocurrency fraud, and sign international agreements to allow swift cooperation across borders. Banks, email providers and online platforms should be required to monitor suspicious activity, alert customers and share information about attacks. Public awareness campaigns, similar to those used against scam phone calls, would help citizens recognise the most common tricks.

In conclusion, cybercrime has grown because opportunities and tools have expanded faster than defences. Coordinated action from individuals, governments and companies is the only realistic response.
key_ideas: Cause 1: more of life online = more targets
Cause 2: AI enables cheap sophisticated scams
Cause 3: weak passwords and poor security habits
Cause 4: cross-border investigation difficult
Solution 1: digital hygiene (2FA, password managers)
Solution 2: specialised police and updated laws
Solution 3: international cooperation treaties
Solution 4: banks and platforms monitor + alert

question_text: Food waste has become a major global issue, with a significant proportion of produced food thrown away every year. What are the main causes of this problem, and what can be done to reduce food waste?
sample_band_7_answer:
Roughly one-third of all food produced globally is wasted each year — a scandal both ethically and environmentally, given that hundreds of millions of people still go hungry. Identifying why so much edible food is lost, and what steps can reverse the trend, is therefore crucial.

Food waste has several distinct causes. At the farm and supply-chain level, fresh produce is often rejected by supermarkets for purely cosmetic reasons — a slightly bent cucumber or an apple with a spot is discarded even though it is perfectly edible. Poor cold storage and transport infrastructure in many countries also mean that substantial quantities spoil before reaching consumers. At the retail level, supermarkets overstock shelves to look attractive to customers, and unsold food near its sell-by date is routinely thrown out. In households, consumers buy more than they can use, misunderstand 'best before' dates and fail to plan meals carefully, leading to waste at home.

Fortunately, several practical solutions can reduce the problem significantly. At the supply-chain level, retailers should accept so-called 'ugly produce' and sell it at discounted prices, as several European supermarkets now do. Improving cold storage and reducing the distance food travels would also cut losses in transit. Governments can pass laws that encourage supermarkets to donate unsold food to charities rather than throwing it away — France, for example, banned large retailers from destroying edible leftovers.

Consumers bear responsibility too. Simple habits such as planning weekly meals, checking the fridge before shopping, storing food properly and understanding the difference between 'best before' (quality) and 'use by' (safety) can dramatically reduce household waste. Apps that connect customers with leftover meals from cafés and restaurants, such as Too Good To Go, have also proved popular. Finally, schools can teach young people to cook simple meals and value food rather than discard it.

In conclusion, food waste is a shared problem with shared solutions. Smarter supply chains, supportive laws and informed consumers can together cut waste substantially.
key_ideas: Cause 1: cosmetic rejection of imperfect produce
Cause 2: poor cold storage and transport
Cause 3: supermarket overstocking and disposal
Cause 4: household over-buying and label confusion
Solution 1: 'ugly produce' discounts
Solution 2: laws requiring supermarket donations (France)
Solution 3: meal planning and label education
Solution 4: surplus-food apps like Too Good To Go

question_text: International tourism has grown rapidly in recent decades, but in some popular destinations it has begun to damage local cultures and environments. What problems does mass tourism cause, and what can be done to address them?
sample_band_7_answer:
International tourism brings economic benefits to many countries, yet in popular destinations the sheer scale of visitors has started to cause serious harm. Understanding these problems and designing effective responses has become urgent for governments and local communities alike.

Mass tourism creates a range of difficulties. Environmentally, popular sites such as beaches, coral reefs and historic city centres suffer from pollution, erosion and overuse. Low-cost flights have also become a significant source of carbon emissions. Socially, rents in tourist-heavy neighbourhoods rise sharply when apartments are converted to short-term holiday lets, pushing out local residents — a pattern clearly seen in Barcelona, Venice and Kyoto. Culturally, authentic traditions can become staged performances for visitors, losing their deeper meaning. Infrastructure designed for a far smaller population — water supply, sewage, transport — is stretched to its limits during peak seasons.

Several measures can address these problems without eliminating tourism's benefits. Governments can limit the number of daily visitors to fragile sites through permit systems, as Machu Picchu and some Croatian islands have done. Tourist taxes, common in European cities, raise funds for conservation and infrastructure while modestly discouraging overcrowding. Regulation of short-term rental platforms, combined with zoning that protects residential neighbourhoods, can slow the displacement of local people.

Airlines and tour operators can be required to invest in carbon-offset schemes, and public awareness campaigns can encourage travellers to visit less crowded destinations or travel in off-peak seasons. Local communities should also share directly in tourist revenue, so that residents see the industry as an ally rather than a threat. Educating visitors about respectful behaviour — dressing appropriately at religious sites, respecting photography rules — helps protect cultural heritage.

In conclusion, mass tourism causes environmental, social and cultural damage that must be taken seriously. Careful management, rather than abandoning the industry, is the realistic and effective response.
key_ideas: Problem 1: environmental damage to fragile sites
Problem 2: rising rents and displacement (Barcelona, Venice)
Problem 3: culture becomes staged for tourists
Problem 4: infrastructure overload
Solution 1: visitor permit limits
Solution 2: tourist taxes for conservation
Solution 3: regulate short-term rentals
Solution 4: visitor education and off-peak incentives

question_text: In many countries, young people show very little interest in politics and rarely take part in elections. What are the main causes of this trend, and what solutions could address it?
sample_band_7_answer:
In many democracies around the world, young people show much lower levels of political engagement than older generations and often fail to vote in elections. This trend raises serious concerns for the future of democratic government and deserves close attention.

Several factors explain young people's disengagement. The first is a widespread belief that politicians do not represent their interests. Many parties focus on issues important to older voters, such as pensions and property, while topics young people care most about — housing affordability, climate change and job insecurity — receive less attention. Second, formal politics often feels distant, bureaucratic and slow, especially compared with the fast feedback of social media. Third, schools in many countries provide little practical education about how democracy works, so young people leave education without understanding how political decisions affect their lives. Finally, a lack of young candidates and role models makes politics feel like something that belongs to older generations.

There are several realistic solutions. Schools can play a major role by teaching civic education from an early age, including practical activities such as school councils and mock elections. Voter-registration campaigns and automatic registration at eighteen, already used in some countries, make participation easier. Lowering the voting age to sixteen, as done in Austria and Scotland, has been shown to build lifelong voting habits.

Politicians themselves must also act. They should meet young voters in schools, universities and on social-media platforms rather than expecting the young to come to traditional rallies. More importantly, parties should take youth concerns seriously in their policies, especially on climate, housing and education costs. Finally, encouraging young people to stand as candidates, through mentoring programmes and fair funding rules, would show that politics belongs to everyone.

In conclusion, young people's political disengagement has understandable causes but effective remedies. Better education, easier participation and more responsive politicians can rebuild democratic engagement.
key_ideas: Cause 1: parties focus on older-voter issues
Cause 2: politics feels slow vs social media
Cause 3: weak civic education in schools
Cause 4: few young candidates or role models
Solution 1: civic education + school councils
Solution 2: automatic registration + lower voting age
Solution 3: politicians engage via social media
Solution 4: support young candidates

question_text: Fake news and misinformation spread rapidly through social media and other online platforms. What are the main causes of this problem, and what can be done to limit its effects?
sample_band_7_answer:
False information has always existed, but social media has enabled it to spread further and faster than ever before, with serious consequences for public health, elections and social cohesion. Understanding why misinformation thrives online and how it can be countered is therefore a pressing issue.

Several factors drive the rapid spread of fake news. Social-media algorithms are designed to maximise engagement, and sensational or emotionally charged content, whether true or false, attracts more clicks than accurate but neutral reporting. A second cause is the collapse of traditional gatekeepers: in previous decades, editors verified facts before publication, but today anyone can reach a large audience with no check at all. Third, many users lack the media literacy skills needed to evaluate sources, so they accept a striking headline without reading further. Finally, deliberate actors — including foreign governments and political activists — have learned to exploit these weaknesses, sometimes using automated bot accounts to amplify false stories.

Combating misinformation requires action at several levels. Platforms should redesign their algorithms to reduce the spread of unverified content and label disputed posts clearly. Requiring transparency about political advertising and the use of automated accounts would also help. Governments can introduce sensible regulation — the European Union's Digital Services Act is one example — without falling into censorship by targeting only clearly false or dangerous content.

Schools have a crucial role too. Media-literacy lessons can teach students to check sources, verify images and recognise emotional manipulation. Independent fact-checking organisations should be supported through public funding and integrated into search results and social feeds. Finally, individuals bear responsibility: pausing before sharing, reading beyond the headline and questioning posts that trigger strong emotions can all slow misinformation at its source.

In conclusion, fake news spreads because of platform design, weak gatekeepers and poor media literacy. A combination of smarter algorithms, sensible regulation and better-educated users can significantly reduce its harm.
key_ideas: Cause 1: algorithms reward sensational content
Cause 2: collapse of editorial gatekeeping
Cause 3: poor media literacy
Cause 4: deliberate bots and foreign actors
Solution 1: platform redesign + labelling
Solution 2: transparent ad + bot rules (EU DSA)
Solution 3: media literacy in schools
Solution 4: fact-checkers and personal responsibility

question_text: Many people today use social media as their main way of maintaining friendships. What are the advantages and disadvantages of relying on social media for friendship?
sample_band_7_answer:
Social media has become the default way in which millions of people stay in contact with friends. This trend brings clear advantages but also meaningful drawbacks that deserve careful attention.

The most obvious advantage of using social media for friendship is convenience. A single post or message can update dozens of friends at once, which is especially valuable for people with busy lives or large social circles. Platforms also make it easy to maintain relationships across long distances, so that friendships formed at school or university can survive even when people move abroad. In addition, social media allows shy or introverted individuals to express themselves at their own pace, without the pressure of face-to-face interaction. Users can also rediscover old friends who might otherwise have been lost entirely.

However, there are important disadvantages. Relationships maintained purely through likes and short comments can become shallow. A 'happy birthday' message takes only a second to type and requires no real emotional investment, so people may feel connected without actually being close. Social media also tends to show carefully edited versions of others' lives, which can trigger feelings of inadequacy, jealousy or loneliness — especially in younger users. Arguments that would once have been resolved in person now often play out through angry messages that damage relationships further. Finally, the constant stream of posts encourages many people to check their phones obsessively, reducing the time they spend in real conversation with family and friends.

In conclusion, social media makes it easier than ever to keep in touch, but the quality of those connections is often weaker than in-person friendship. The best approach is to use online platforms to stay informed and arrange real meetings, rather than treating digital contact as a complete substitute for face-to-face time.
key_ideas: ADV 1: convenience, one post reaches many
ADV 2: long-distance friendships survive
ADV 3: suits introverted people
ADV 4: rediscover lost friends
DIS 1: shallow interactions (likes/comments)
DIS 2: edited feeds trigger loneliness/jealousy
DIS 3: online arguments escalate
DIS 4: constant phone-checking reduces real contact

question_text: More and more students today choose to study at universities abroad rather than in their own country. What are the advantages and disadvantages of studying abroad?
sample_band_7_answer:
An increasing number of students now pursue their university education in a foreign country rather than at home. This trend offers considerable opportunities but also involves real costs that prospective students must weigh carefully.

The advantages of studying abroad can be substantial. Academically, foreign universities often provide access to programmes, facilities or teaching methods that are unavailable at home, and a degree from a well-known international institution can open career doors for life. Students typically improve their language skills rapidly through daily exposure, gaining a level of fluency that classroom study alone rarely achieves. Living independently in another culture also builds maturity, adaptability and problem-solving ability — qualities that employers increasingly value. Finally, the international network of friends and professional contacts built during such studies often proves useful throughout a graduate's career.

There are, however, significant disadvantages. The financial cost is usually the most obvious: tuition, accommodation and travel for an international student can amount to several times the cost of studying at home, and many families take on substantial debt. Homesickness and cultural isolation are also common, particularly during the first year, and can affect mental health and academic performance. Students who do not share the language of their host country may initially struggle with lectures and coursework, which can damage their grades. There is also the risk of 'brain drain': if the most talented students settle permanently abroad, their home country loses the benefit of their skills.

In my view, whether the advantages outweigh the disadvantages depends on individual circumstances. For students with strong language skills, adequate funding and clear career goals, studying abroad is usually a transformative experience. For those without these advantages, a good domestic university combined with shorter exchange programmes may deliver many of the same benefits at lower cost.

In conclusion, studying abroad offers real rewards in education, language and personal growth, but at a significant financial and emotional price.
key_ideas: ADV 1: access to unique programmes and facilities
ADV 2: rapid language improvement through immersion
ADV 3: builds maturity and adaptability
ADV 4: international professional network
DIS 1: very expensive
DIS 2: homesickness and cultural isolation
DIS 3: language challenges hurt grades initially
DIS 4: brain drain from home country

question_text: Large international companies often open branches or factories in developing countries. What are the advantages and disadvantages of this for the host country?
sample_band_7_answer:
Large multinational companies increasingly operate in developing countries, setting up factories, offices and retail outlets. For the host country, this trend brings noticeable benefits but also a number of risks.

The most obvious advantage is job creation. A new factory or office can employ thousands of local people directly, and many more indirectly through suppliers and services. These jobs often pay higher wages than the local average, allowing families to improve their living standards. Multinational companies also bring new technology and management practices, which local staff learn on the job and may later apply in domestic businesses. Foreign investment boosts the country's export earnings and encourages the government to improve roads, ports and electricity supply to attract further business.

However, the presence of multinationals also has disadvantages. Profits generated in the host country often flow back to shareholders abroad rather than being reinvested locally. Small, locally owned businesses may struggle to compete with the prices and brand recognition of global rivals, leading to closures. Some multinational operations have also been criticised for poor working conditions, long hours and low wages relative to the company's home country, particularly in the garment and electronics industries. Environmental damage is another common concern, as foreign companies sometimes take advantage of weaker regulation to pollute more freely than they would at home.

Perhaps the most serious long-term risk is economic dependence. If a developing country relies too heavily on a few foreign employers, a sudden withdrawal can cause mass unemployment and economic shock.

In conclusion, multinational companies bring jobs, technology and investment that poorer countries badly need, but they can also crowd out local businesses, exploit workers and damage the environment. The key is effective regulation, so that host countries capture the benefits while limiting the harms.
key_ideas: ADV 1: job creation and higher wages
ADV 2: technology and management skills transferred
ADV 3: infrastructure improvement
ADV 4: export earnings
DIS 1: profits leave the country
DIS 2: local businesses out-competed
DIS 3: poor working conditions possible
DIS 4: environmental damage and economic dependence

question_text: A growing number of people today prefer to work as freelancers or to be self-employed rather than take a permanent job. What are the advantages and disadvantages of this kind of work compared to a permanent job?
sample_band_7_answer:
Freelancing and self-employment have grown significantly in recent years, helped by digital platforms that connect workers with clients around the world. For many people, this style of work is attractive, but it involves trade-offs that differ noticeably from those of a permanent job.

The advantages of freelancing are considerable. The most immediate is flexibility: freelancers decide when, where and how much they work, which allows them to combine career and family, travel or further study far more easily than in a traditional job. Successful freelancers often earn more per hour than employees because they keep the full value of their work rather than sharing it with an employer. They can also choose which clients and projects to accept, which gives greater variety and helps avoid burnout. Finally, freelancers are their own boss, and for independently minded people this autonomy is itself a major reward.

However, the disadvantages of freelance work are equally real. Income is unpredictable: busy months may be followed by weeks with no work at all, which can make budgeting and long-term planning difficult. Freelancers usually pay for their own health insurance, pension and equipment, benefits that a permanent employer would normally cover. When they are ill or take a holiday, they simply earn nothing. Isolation is also common, as many freelancers work alone at home without the daily contact and mentoring that an office provides. The constant need to find new clients, negotiate prices and chase late payments adds a layer of stress that permanent employees rarely experience.

Permanent jobs, in contrast, offer predictable salaries, paid leave, training, social connections and clearer career paths, at the cost of less flexibility.

In conclusion, freelancing delivers freedom and potentially higher earnings but also instability and loneliness, while a permanent job provides security and structure with less independence. The right choice depends on each individual's personality, life stage and financial situation.
key_ideas: ADV 1: flexibility in time, location, workload
ADV 2: higher per-hour earnings possible
ADV 3: choice of clients and variety
ADV 4: autonomy and being one's own boss
DIS 1: unpredictable income
DIS 2: no employer-paid benefits or paid leave
DIS 3: isolation and no mentoring
DIS 4: client-hunting and late payments add stress

question_text: Many people today move to large cities in search of better opportunities, while others prefer the peace of small towns or rural areas. What are the advantages and disadvantages of living in a large city compared with a small town?
sample_band_7_answer:
Rapid urbanisation continues across most of the world, with millions of people leaving small towns for major cities each year. Although city life offers significant opportunities, it also involves drawbacks that smaller communities usually avoid.

The advantages of living in a large city are obvious. Job opportunities are by far the most important: cities concentrate industries, company headquarters and start-ups, giving residents access to positions that simply do not exist in small towns. Education, too, is generally superior, with better-funded schools, leading universities and specialist training centres. Healthcare in large cities tends to be more advanced, and specialist hospitals treat conditions that rural facilities cannot. Beyond work and study, cities offer an unmatched range of cultural activities — theatres, concerts, museums and restaurants from around the world — as well as diverse communities in which people from different backgrounds can find like-minded friends. Public transport in most major cities also reduces the need for private cars.

Small towns, however, have their own undeniable advantages that reveal the disadvantages of urban life. The pace is slower, the air cleaner and the stress lower. Housing is usually far more affordable, and people often own larger homes with gardens. Communities tend to be closer: neighbours know each other, children grow up in safer streets and older residents are rarely as isolated as they can be in anonymous city apartments. In contrast, big cities suffer from severe traffic congestion, noise, pollution and extreme housing costs, which can leave even well-paid residents struggling financially. Crime rates are usually higher, and many people report feeling lonely despite being surrounded by millions of others.

In conclusion, large cities offer unmatched opportunities in work, study and culture, but they come with real costs in terms of stress, expense and community. Small towns trade those opportunities for a calmer, cheaper and more connected life. The best choice depends on each person's priorities.
key_ideas: ADV city 1: concentrated job opportunities
ADV city 2: better education and healthcare
ADV city 3: cultural variety and diverse community
ADV city 4: public transport reduces car need
DIS city 1: traffic, noise, pollution
DIS city 2: expensive housing
DIS city 3: higher crime rates
DIS city 4: loneliness despite crowds

question_text: A growing number of people now get their news from social media rather than from traditional newspapers or television. What are the advantages and disadvantages of getting news from social media?
sample_band_7_answer:
Social media has become one of the main sources of news for many people, especially younger users. Although getting news this way has clear advantages, it also brings significant drawbacks that deserve attention.

The advantages of social-media news are obvious. First, information arrives almost instantly: breaking events, from natural disasters to political developments, appear on timelines within minutes, long before traditional newspapers or TV bulletins can report them. Second, social media is free and easily accessible, which is particularly important for people on low incomes who cannot afford a newspaper subscription. Third, users encounter a wider variety of viewpoints. They can read opinions from journalists, experts, witnesses on the ground and ordinary citizens — voices that traditional newsrooms might never reach. Finally, social-media news allows direct interaction: readers can ask questions, add their own photos or respond to reporters, which creates a more participatory experience.

However, there are also serious disadvantages. The most significant is the risk of misinformation. Unlike traditional newsrooms, social-media posts usually go through no fact-checking process, and rumours or outright lies can spread faster than accurate reports. Second, platforms use algorithms that show users content similar to what they have already liked, creating 'filter bubbles' in which people only encounter views that confirm their beliefs. Third, social-media news is often sensationalised to attract attention, with emotional headlines replacing careful analysis. Finally, advertising and sponsored content can be hard to distinguish from genuine journalism, making it easy to mislead casual readers.

In conclusion, social media makes news faster, freer and more varied than ever before, but it also exposes readers to misinformation, bias and manipulation. The wisest approach is to use social media alongside traditional sources rather than as a complete replacement.
key_ideas: ADV 1: instant news delivery
ADV 2: free and accessible
ADV 3: diverse voices and viewpoints
ADV 4: interactive and participatory
DIS 1: no fact-checking, misinformation spreads
DIS 2: algorithmic filter bubbles
DIS 3: sensationalism over analysis
DIS 4: blurred line between ads and news

question_text: Some people argue that it would be better if the whole world used a single common language for communication. What are the advantages and disadvantages of having one global language?
sample_band_7_answer:
The idea of a single global language has appeared at different points in history, and in practice English now performs that role in many areas of international life. Whether a universally shared language would be a good thing, however, is a genuinely mixed question with important arguments on both sides.

The advantages of one common language would be substantial. International business would operate far more efficiently when all contracts, meetings and negotiations used the same tongue, reducing misunderstandings and translation costs. Scientific progress would also accelerate: researchers would read each other's work directly, without waiting for translation, and collaboration across borders would become far easier. Travel would become simpler for ordinary people, who could ask directions, book hotels and make friends anywhere without language barriers. International cooperation on issues such as climate change, pandemic response or refugee crises would benefit enormously when representatives could speak directly to one another rather than through interpreters.

However, the disadvantages are equally serious. Language is not simply a tool; it carries history, literature, humour and identity that cannot always be translated. If all children grew up speaking a single global language, thousands of smaller languages could disappear within a few generations, taking with them unique ways of seeing the world. Native speakers of the dominant language — most likely English — would also enjoy an unfair advantage in education, business and science, while others would forever operate in a second language. Many cultural traditions, from songs to jokes to poetry, would lose something essential when forced into another tongue. Finally, a single language might create a dangerous sense that all cultures should think alike, reducing the diversity that has driven human creativity.

In conclusion, a global language would make communication easier but at a serious cost to linguistic and cultural diversity. Bilingualism — keeping local languages alongside a shared second language — offers most of the benefits without the harm.
key_ideas: ADV 1: efficient international business
ADV 2: faster scientific progress
ADV 3: easier travel
ADV 4: smoother global cooperation on major issues
DIS 1: loss of thousands of smaller languages
DIS 2: unfair advantage for native speakers
DIS 3: cultural expressions lost in translation
DIS 4: threatens diversity of thought

question_text: Today, people have easy access to large amounts of health information through the internet. What are the advantages and disadvantages of this for ordinary people?
sample_band_7_answer:
The internet has dramatically changed the way ordinary people engage with health. Where once a visit to a doctor was the only way to understand symptoms or treatment options, patients can now consult a vast online library in seconds. This access brings genuine benefits but also real risks, which must be weighed carefully.

The advantages are considerable. Patients can research symptoms and conditions in advance of a medical appointment, which allows them to ask more informed questions and understand the doctor's advice more fully. For people living with chronic diseases, online resources and peer communities provide emotional support and practical tips that a short consultation cannot match. The internet also gives access to medical knowledge that was previously locked inside professional journals: credible sources such as the Mayo Clinic, the NHS and WHO offer clear, evidence-based information to anyone in minutes. In remote areas without a nearby specialist, telehealth platforms combined with trustworthy online information can noticeably improve outcomes. Prevention is strengthened too, as patients read about healthy lifestyles, screenings and vaccinations.

However, the disadvantages can be equally serious. The quality of health information online varies enormously, and patients often struggle to distinguish a peer-reviewed medical site from a commercial blog trying to sell supplements. Excessive self-diagnosis is also a well-documented problem, with anxious readers convinced they have a rare cancer after researching a mild symptom — a phenomenon sometimes called 'cyberchondria'. At the opposite extreme, some users may dismiss serious warnings they have read and delay professional treatment, which can prove dangerous. Misinformation about vaccines, alternative therapies or 'miracle cures' has real consequences for public health, as recent outbreaks of preventable diseases have shown.

In my view, online health information is most valuable when used as a supplement to professional care, not a replacement. Patients who combine reliable online research with genuine medical consultation gain understanding without unnecessary fear — but only if schools and doctors actively teach them how to evaluate what they read.
key_ideas: ADV 1: prepared for medical appointments
ADV 2: peer support for chronic conditions
ADV 3: access to expert-grade information (Mayo, NHS, WHO)
ADV 4: prevention and rural access improved
DIS 1: variable quality of sources
DIS 2: self-diagnosis causes anxiety (cyberchondria)
DIS 3: users dismiss real warnings
DIS 4: vaccine and 'cure' misinformation

question_text: Some people believe that renewable energy sources such as solar and wind will eventually replace fossil fuels. Do you agree with this prediction? What are the main obstacles preventing a faster transition to renewable energy?
sample_band_7_answer:
Whether renewable energy will one day replace fossil fuels is one of the defining questions of our time. In my view, a near-complete transition is both likely and necessary, although several serious obstacles are slowing its progress.

I broadly agree that renewables will eventually dominate global energy supply. The cost of solar panels has fallen by more than 90 per cent in the last fifteen years, and onshore wind power is now the cheapest source of new electricity in many countries. These technologies also avoid the volatile fuel prices associated with oil and gas. Governments around the world — from the European Union to China — have committed to binding climate targets that simply cannot be met without a dramatic reduction in fossil-fuel use. Combined with rising public demand for clean air and climate action, these pressures make the long-term direction of travel clear, even if the speed of change is debated.

However, the obstacles to faster progress are real and significant. The first is the intermittency of solar and wind: the sun does not always shine, nor the wind blow, so large-scale battery storage or flexible backup sources are required. Battery technology is improving rapidly but remains expensive. Second, many electricity grids were designed decades ago around coal and gas plants and are not yet suited to a decentralised renewable system; rebuilding them demands huge investment and political coordination. Third, fossil-fuel industries have powerful political influence and often receive subsidies that slow the playing field. In developing countries, the upfront capital cost of renewable infrastructure is particularly difficult to finance without international support. Finally, some sectors — such as aviation, shipping and heavy industry — are genuinely hard to electrify and may require new fuels such as green hydrogen, which are still at an early stage.

In conclusion, renewables will almost certainly replace most fossil-fuel use in the coming decades, but only if governments confront the political, financial and technical barriers now standing in the way.
key_ideas: AGREE: solar/wind cheapest new electricity
AGREE: EU/China binding climate targets
AGREE: rising public demand
OBSTACLE 1: intermittency requires battery storage
OBSTACLE 2: outdated grid infrastructure
OBSTACLE 3: fossil-fuel political influence and subsidies
OBSTACLE 4: hard-to-electrify sectors (aviation, shipping)
OBSTACLE 5: financing in developing countries

question_text: Children in many countries are spending increasing amounts of time on screen-based activities such as watching videos and playing games. Why is this trend happening, and what effects might it have on children's development?
sample_band_7_answer:
Across most of the world, children are spending far more hours in front of screens than any previous generation. This shift has clear causes and potentially significant consequences for their development, both positive and negative.

Several factors explain the rising screen time. The most obvious is the enormous expansion of digital content specifically designed to hold children's attention, from YouTube videos to mobile games with reward loops borrowed from gambling psychology. Second, many parents rely on screens as convenient childcare, particularly in households where both adults work long hours. Third, outdoor play has become less common as urban environments grow denser, traffic busier and parents more anxious about safety. Finally, school and homework have moved partly online, meaning even academically diligent children now spend time on devices. During and after the pandemic, these trends accelerated sharply.

The effects on children's development are mixed but increasingly concerning. On the positive side, well-designed educational apps can genuinely teach languages, mathematics or coding, and video calls allow children to maintain relationships with distant relatives. Creative tools, from digital art programmes to simple coding platforms, have given young people new ways to express themselves.

However, the risks are substantial. Heavy screen use in early childhood has been linked to delayed language development, shorter attention spans and weaker social skills. Physical effects include poorer sleep, rising rates of short-sightedness and less time spent in active play, which harms motor development and fitness. Social-media exposure in adolescence correlates with anxiety, low self-esteem and disrupted sleep, particularly among girls. Perhaps most worryingly, the algorithmic design of many apps can encourage compulsive use, making it harder for children to self-regulate.

In conclusion, children are spending more time on screens because digital content is abundant, outdoor alternatives have shrunk, and learning itself is now partly digital. The effects depend strongly on the type of content and the presence of clear limits, making adult guidance essential.
key_ideas: CAUSE 1: content designed to capture attention
CAUSE 2: screens as convenient childcare
CAUSE 3: less outdoor play in cities
CAUSE 4: digital learning in schools
POSITIVE EFFECT: educational apps, creative tools
NEGATIVE EFFECT 1: delayed language and attention
NEGATIVE EFFECT 2: poor sleep, short-sightedness
NEGATIVE EFFECT 3: anxiety in adolescent girls

question_text: More people in many countries are choosing to live alone rather than with family members or partners. Why is this trend happening? Is this a positive or negative development for society?
sample_band_7_answer:
In a growing number of countries, the single-person household is now the most common living arrangement. Understanding why this is happening and whether it is good or bad for society is therefore important.

Several factors explain the rise in solo living. First, people marry much later than in the past, or never marry at all, so they spend many more years of adult life alone. Higher incomes and greater personal independence, particularly for women, allow young adults to afford their own apartments rather than remain with parents. Second, divorce rates have increased, leaving more middle-aged adults living on their own. Third, populations are ageing, so more elderly people now live alone after losing a partner, especially in countries where children no longer live with their parents. Finally, the rise of remote work has made solo living more practical, as a home becomes both a workplace and a refuge.

Whether this trend is positive or negative depends on how we look at it. On the positive side, living alone offers privacy, personal freedom and the ability to shape one's schedule entirely around one's own goals. Many adults find this liberating, and it often encourages personal growth, self-reliance and the development of a wider circle of friends outside the household. Economically, solo living supports demand for housing, furniture and household goods, which contributes to economic activity.

On the negative side, solo living can increase loneliness, especially among older adults, and is associated with higher rates of depression and poorer health outcomes. Cities need far more small homes, which can worsen already-tight housing markets. Lower birth rates, common in countries with many solo households, can also strain pension and healthcare systems as populations age.

In conclusion, people are choosing solo living because of later marriage, divorce, ageing and independence. The trend brings both freedom and risks, making it a mixed rather than clearly positive or negative development.
key_ideas: CAUSE 1: marry later / never marry
CAUSE 2: women's financial independence
CAUSE 3: higher divorce rates
CAUSE 4: ageing populations and remote work
POSITIVE: privacy, freedom, personal growth
POSITIVE: drives housing/consumer demand
NEGATIVE: loneliness and depression in elderly
NEGATIVE: housing shortages and low birth rates

question_text: Some people argue that students should be taught practical life skills at school, such as cooking, personal finance and first aid. Why do some people argue for this? Do you think schools should teach these skills?
sample_band_7_answer:
Many educators and parents now argue that schools should teach practical life skills alongside traditional academic subjects. This debate has grown sharper as young adults increasingly report feeling unprepared for daily life, and it raises genuine questions about the purpose of modern education.

Those who call for more practical teaching have clear reasons. The first is that academic subjects, while important, leave many teenagers unable to perform basic adult tasks. Surveys in several countries show that substantial numbers of eighteen-year-olds cannot prepare a simple meal, read a pay slip or respond to a medical emergency. Second, traditional teachers of these skills — parents and grandparents — have less time or knowledge than in previous generations; many young people grow up in busy households where nobody cooks, and financial products have become more complex. Third, supporters point to the personal cost of these gaps: poor financial decisions can lead to debt, unhealthy eating habits contribute to obesity, and lack of first-aid knowledge costs lives every year. Teaching such skills in schools, they argue, would reach every child equally, regardless of home background.

I broadly agree that schools should include practical skills in the curriculum, although not at the expense of academic learning. A short weekly lesson from primary school to the end of secondary school could realistically cover cooking, budgeting, tax basics, first aid, digital safety and mental-health awareness. Many countries, including Finland and Japan, already do this with positive results: Japanese children traditionally learn to cook and clean classrooms, which builds responsibility as well as skills.

Of course, there are legitimate concerns. Adding new subjects competes for limited timetable space, and teachers need proper training to deliver them well. However, these are implementation challenges rather than reasons to reject the idea.

In conclusion, teaching practical life skills addresses a real gap and prepares young people for adult life. Schools should find room for them alongside academic subjects.
key_ideas: ARG 1: teens can't do basic adult tasks
ARG 2: parents pass on less these days
ARG 3: lack of skills causes real harm (debt, obesity, emergencies)
ARG 4: schools reach every child equally
POSITION: yes, include short weekly lessons
EXAMPLE: Finland and Japan already do this
CONCERN: timetable space and teacher training
CONCLUSION: alongside academic subjects, not instead

question_text: In many countries, public trust in governments has fallen significantly in recent decades. Why has this trust declined, and what can governments do to rebuild public confidence?
sample_band_7_answer:
Survey after survey shows that trust in national governments has dropped sharply in many democracies over the past three decades. Understanding why this has happened, and what can realistically restore confidence, is crucial for the future of democratic life.

Several factors explain the decline. The first is a long series of visible failures and scandals — financial crises, unjustified wars, cases of corruption and inadequate responses to emergencies — each of which has eroded the public's belief that politicians can be counted on to act in the national interest. Second, economic changes have left many voters feeling worse off. Wages have stagnated for decades in many countries while the cost of housing, healthcare and education has risen, creating a widespread sense that governments serve the wealthy rather than ordinary citizens. Third, the fragmentation of the media means that opposition politicians, tabloid outlets and social-media accounts now amplify every mistake, making past decades' politics look honest by comparison — often unfairly. Finally, growing polarisation means that supporters of the losing side in any election increasingly distrust the winners as a matter of principle.

Rebuilding trust is difficult but possible. Governments should, above all, deliver visible improvements in everyday life — affordable housing, shorter healthcare waits and reliable public transport — because real results count for far more than rhetoric. They should also commit to transparency: publishing government spending, ministerial meetings and policy impact assessments builds confidence that decisions are taken for legitimate reasons. Strong, independent institutions such as anti-corruption agencies, free press and impartial courts deter and expose wrongdoing. Inclusive decision-making, in which citizens are consulted through assemblies and public hearings on major issues, gives people a stake in outcomes even when they disagree with the final result. Finally, politicians can rebuild personal credibility simply by admitting mistakes rather than denying them.

In conclusion, public trust has fallen because of real failures, economic frustration and a more critical media environment. Governments can restore it by delivering results, embracing transparency and strengthening independent institutions.
key_ideas: CAUSE 1: scandals and policy failures
CAUSE 2: economic stagnation for ordinary voters
CAUSE 3: fragmented media amplifies mistakes
CAUSE 4: political polarisation
SOLUTION 1: deliver visible daily-life improvements
SOLUTION 2: transparency and open data
SOLUTION 3: independent institutions and free press
SOLUTION 4: citizen assemblies and admitting errors
